enrichment.short_description: Open-source, event-driven orchestration platform that lets teams build data, AI and infrastructure workflows with YAML and 1,200+ plugins.
enrichment.seo_title: Kestra Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kestra review covering features, pricing tiers, plugins and how it compares to Airflow, Prefect and Dagster for workflow orchestration.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kestra is an open-source, event-driven orchestration platform founded in 2021 in Paris, France by Emmanuel Darras, a co-founder of the gaming studio Ankama, together with CTO Ludovic Dehon. The company, formally Kestra Technologies, has raised roughly 31 million euros (about 36 million dollars) in total funding, including a 3 million dollar pre-seed round in 2023, an 8 million dollar seed round in September 2024, and a 21 million euro (about 25 million dollar) Series A led by RTP Global with participation from Alven, ISAI and Axeleo, announced in March 2026. Kestra reports serving around 30,000 organizations globally and executing more than 2 billion workflow runs in 2025, a twentyfold increase year over year, with enterprise users reportedly including Apple, Toyota, JPMorgan Chase, BHP, Bloomberg, Credit Agricole and Xiaomi. Architecturally, Kestra is a declarative, YAML-based orchestration engine that treats workflows as code while also offering a visual, UI-based flow builder, positioning itself as a polyglot alternative to Python-only tools such as Apache Airflow and Prefect. It supports both scheduled and event-driven triggers, meaning flows can react to webhooks, message queues, file arrivals or API calls rather than only running on a cron schedule. The platform ships with more than 1,200 plugins covering databases, cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure), data warehouses, dbt, Python, container and Kubernetes execution, AI and LLM tasks, and general-purpose scripting in nearly any language, letting a single flow orchestrate tasks written in different languages and runtimes. Kestra can be self-hosted for free as fully open-source software with unlimited flows and executions, deployed on-premises, in a private cloud, or in air-gapped environments. Kestra Cloud is the company's fully managed SaaS offering, billed on a pay-as-you-consume usage basis, and includes automatic scaling, built-in security and governance, enterprise plugins, and SOC2 compliance without the customer needing to operate infrastructure. The Enterprise Edition is a self-managed or private-cloud deployment sold under an annual per-instance subscription with custom, quote-based pricing that scales with organization size and required features; it layers on LDAP, SCIM and custom role-based access control, single sign-on and public-cloud authentication integrations, audit logs, storage isolation, multi-tenancy, worker groups and dedicated task runners, external secret manager integrations, enterprise-exclusive plugins, an AI Copilot with support for custom models, and a customer success program with SLA-backed support (Standard by default, with Premium and Platinum tiers available). Kestra targets data engineering, platform engineering and DevOps teams inside mid-market and large enterprises who need to orchestrate ETL/ELT pipelines, machine learning workflows, business process automation, and infrastructure tasks across heterogeneous tech stacks without being locked into a single programming language. Its most direct competitors are Apache Airflow, whose Python-DAG-first model is mature but can be harder to version and roll back and is less friendly to non-Python teams; Prefect, which offers a strong Python developer experience via decorators but is fundamentally Python-only; Dagster, which takes an asset-centric approach optimized for data lineage, testability and dbt-heavy pipelines rather than general task orchestration; and Astronomer, a managed-Airflow vendor. Kestra differentiates itself by combining a declarative, language-agnostic workflow definition format with native event-driven triggering, a large first-party plugin catalog, and a tiered path from free self-hosted open source through managed cloud to enterprise governance features, aiming to reduce the operational complexity that comes with running and scaling traditional orchestrators like Airflow at enterprise volume.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kestra is an open-source orchestration platform built to unify data, AI and infrastructure workflows under a single declarative, event-driven engine. Founded in 2021 in Paris by Emmanuel Darras and Ludovic Dehon, the company has grown quickly, raising a Series A round in 2026 that brought total funding to roughly 36 million dollars, and now claims tens of thousands of organizations running billions of workflow executions on the platform annually.

Unlike orchestrators that require workflows to be written exclusively in Python, Kestra defines flows in YAML, which can be authored directly as code or built visually through its UI. This declarative model, combined with support for more than 1,200 plugins spanning cloud providers, databases, scripting languages and AI tooling, lets teams orchestrate polyglot pipelines and react to real-time events rather than only running on fixed schedules.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kestra's core strength is its combination of declarative-as-code workflow definitions with a UI that stays in sync, so teams can edit flows visually or in YAML without drift between the two. Event-driven triggers let workflows kick off from webhooks, queues, file system events or API calls, in addition to traditional cron scheduling, which suits both batch data pipelines and near-real-time automation.

The plugin ecosystem is one of the platform's biggest differentiators, giving out-of-the-box integrations for major cloud platforms, data warehouses, dbt, Kubernetes, containers and general-purpose scripting so a single pipeline can mix languages and tools rather than forcing everything into one framework. For organizations with stricter requirements, the Enterprise Edition adds SSO, LDAP and SCIM-based access control, audit logging, multi-tenancy, worker groups, external secrets managers, and an AI Copilot for building and debugging flows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kestra follows a three-tier model. The open-source edition is free and self-hosted, offering unlimited flows and executions with the full plugin catalog, making it a genuine option for teams comfortable managing their own infrastructure. Kestra Cloud is a fully managed SaaS product billed on usage, removing the operational burden of scaling and patching the orchestrator while adding governance and SOC2-compliant security by default.

The Enterprise Edition targets larger organizations that need self-hosted or private-cloud deployment with enterprise-grade access control, compliance and support; pricing is custom and quote-based, scaling with organization size, deployment footprint and the specific enterprise features required. Prospective enterprise customers typically need to contact Kestra's sales team or book a demo to receive a quote, as detailed pricing is not published publicly.

features.title: Declarative YAML workflows
features.description: Flows are defined as code in YAML, with a synchronized UI editor, avoiding vendor lock-in to a single scripting language.

features.title: Event-driven triggers
features.description: Workflows can start from webhooks, queues, file arrivals or API calls in addition to cron-based scheduling.

features.title: 1,200+ plugins
features.description: Native integrations for cloud providers, databases, dbt, Kubernetes, containers and general scripting languages.

features.title: Polyglot task execution
features.description: A single flow can orchestrate tasks written in different languages and runtimes rather than being Python-only.

features.title: Multi-tenancy and RBAC
features.description: Enterprise Edition supports SSO, LDAP/SCIM, and fine-grained role-based access control for large organizations.

features.title: AI Copilot
features.description: Enterprise-tier assistant for building, debugging and optimizing workflows, with support for custom models.

features.title: Air-gapped and multi-cloud deployment
features.description: Can be self-hosted on-premises, in private cloud, or in air-gapped environments for regulated industries.

features.title: Audit logs and storage isolation
features.description: Enterprise governance features for compliance-sensitive teams running orchestration at scale.

pros: Free, fully-featured open-source self-hosted option with unlimited flows and executions
Language-agnostic declarative YAML model avoids Python lock-in
Large plugin catalog (1,200+) covering most common data and cloud tools
Native event-driven triggers alongside traditional scheduling
Strong recent funding and growing enterprise adoption suggest active development
cons: Enterprise and Cloud pricing are not published and require contacting sales
Younger project than Airflow, so ecosystem maturity and community size are smaller
YAML-based flows can become verbose for very complex pipelines
Enterprise features (SSO, RBAC, audit logs) are gated behind paid tiers
Newer entrant means fewer long-term production case studies compared to Airflow

faqs.question: Is Kestra free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kestra's open-source edition is free to self-host with unlimited flows and executions and the full plugin library. Paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers add managed hosting, governance and enterprise support.

faqs.question: How is Kestra different from Apache Airflow?
faqs.answer: Kestra uses declarative YAML rather than Python DAGs, supports native event-driven triggers, and lets a single flow mix tasks written in different languages, whereas Airflow is Python-first and primarily schedule-driven.

faqs.question: Who founded Kestra?
faqs.answer: Kestra was founded in 2021 by Emmanuel Darras and Ludovic Dehon in Paris, France.

faqs.question: Does Kestra offer a managed cloud version?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kestra Cloud is a fully managed, usage-billed SaaS offering with automatic scaling, built-in security and SOC2 compliance.

faqs.question: What kind of workflows can Kestra orchestrate?
faqs.answer: Kestra can orchestrate data pipelines, ETL/ELT jobs, machine learning workflows, business process automation and infrastructure tasks across cloud and on-premises systems.

faqs.question: How much does Kestra Enterprise Edition cost?
faqs.answer: Enterprise Edition pricing is custom and based on organization size and required features; interested customers need to contact Kestra's sales team for a quote.

use_cases.title: Data pipeline orchestration
use_cases.description: Teams use Kestra to schedule and monitor ETL/ELT pipelines across warehouses, databases and dbt models with full observability.

use_cases.title: Event-driven automation
use_cases.description: Kestra triggers workflows from webhooks, file arrivals or queue messages, enabling near-real-time business process automation.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-grade AI and ML workflow orchestration
use_cases.description: Large organizations run governed, auditable ML and AI pipelines with SSO, RBAC and dedicated worker groups via the Enterprise Edition.

features: Declarative workflows as code and UI-based design
Fully managed hosting, upgrades and monitoring
Worker groups and dedicated task runners

enrichment.short_description: Open-source identity and access management platform providing single sign-on, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect and SAML support for modern applications.
enrichment.seo_title: Keycloak Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keycloak review covering single sign-on, OAuth/OIDC/SAML support, self-hosting, and how it compares to Auth0, Okta and other IAM tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keycloak is an open-source identity and access management (IAM) project that provides single sign-on, user federation, and standards-based authentication for web and mobile applications and APIs. The project was co-founded in 2013 by Stian Thorgersen, a developer at Red Hat, along with colleagues, with its first production release shipping in September 2014 after roughly a year of development. Keycloak began life as a WildFly community project and was steered by Red Hat for years, serving as the upstream open-source project for Red Hat's commercial Red Hat Single Sign-On (RH-SSO) offering, first integrated as the upstream for RH-SSO 7.0 in 2016. In April 2023, Red Hat donated Keycloak to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), where it joined as an incubating project, formalizing vendor-neutral governance while Red Hat continues to ship a commercially supported distribution called Red Hat build of Keycloak. Architecturally, Keycloak acts as a centralized authentication and authorization server that sits in front of an organization's applications: rather than each application implementing its own login and user-management logic, users authenticate once against Keycloak and receive tokens that grant access across integrated apps, implementing true single sign-on and single sign-out. It is built on and fully supports the major identity protocols, including OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0, and SAML 2.0, so it interoperates with a wide range of existing identity providers and client libraries. Keycloak supports identity brokering and social login, allowing organizations to federate authentication through existing OpenID Connect or SAML 2.0 identity providers or through social networks without modifying application code. It also ships with built-in connectors for LDAP and Active Directory, making it straightforward to integrate with existing enterprise directory services rather than migrating user stores. The platform separates concerns between an administrative console, where administrators manage realms, clients, roles, and authorization policies, and a self-service account console, where end users manage their own profile, change passwords, and configure two-factor authentication. Fine-grained authorization services allow policies to be defined per resource and scope, supporting complex permission models beyond simple role-based access control. Because Keycloak is fully open-source (licensed under Apache 2.0) and self-hosted, there is no vendor subscription fee for the software itself; organizations run it on their own infrastructure or via Kubernetes/container deployments, and costs are limited to hosting, operations, and optional commercial support contracts such as those Red Hat sells for its enterprise-supported build. This makes Keycloak the default choice for engineering teams that want full control over their identity infrastructure, need to avoid per-monthly-active-user pricing common among SaaS IAM vendors, or operate in regulated or air-gapped environments where self-hosting is a requirement. Its primary competitors are commercial, usage-priced identity platforms such as Auth0 (owned by Okta), Okta itself, Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD), Ping Identity, and other open-source or self-hostable alternatives like Ory, Authentik and Zitadel. Keycloak differentiates itself from the SaaS incumbents primarily on cost predictability and control, since self-hosting avoids per-user billing, and on protocol completeness and enterprise directory integration, at the cost of requiring the adopting team to operate, patch, and scale the identity server themselves rather than consuming it as a managed service. It is widely used by enterprises, government agencies, and open-source projects that need standards-compliant SSO without recurring per-seat identity costs, and its CNCF incubation status has broadened community contribution and long-term governance beyond any single vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keycloak is an open-source identity and access management platform that provides single sign-on, social login, and user federation for applications and services. Co-founded in 2013 by Stian Thorgersen at Red Hat, with its first production release in September 2014, Keycloak was long stewarded by Red Hat as the upstream project for its commercial Red Hat Single Sign-On product before being donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation in April 2023 as an incubating project.

Rather than requiring each application to implement its own login system, Keycloak centralizes authentication: users sign in once through Keycloak and are issued tokens that grant access across every connected application, implementing genuine single sign-on and single sign-out across an organization's software estate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Keycloak is built on open standards, providing full support for OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0, and SAML 2.0, which means it interoperates with existing identity providers, client libraries, and enterprise systems. It supports identity brokering and social login out of the box, and includes native connectors to LDAP and Active Directory so organizations can federate against an existing user directory instead of migrating accounts.

Administration is split between a centralized admin console for managing realms, roles, clients and authorization policies, and a self-service account console where end users handle their own password changes and two-factor authentication setup. Fine-grained, resource-level authorization policies extend beyond simple role-based access control for organizations with complex permission requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Keycloak itself is fully open-source and free to use under the Apache 2.0 license, with no per-user or per-monthly-active-user licensing fees, since it is designed to be self-hosted on an organization's own infrastructure or Kubernetes cluster. Costs are limited to hosting, operational overhead, and engineering time to deploy, patch, and scale the identity server.

Organizations that want commercial support and a hardened, enterprise-supported distribution can purchase Red Hat build of Keycloak, Red Hat's supported offering built on the upstream project; pricing for that support contract is not published and requires contacting Red Hat directly.

features.title: Single sign-on and single sign-out
features.description: Centralizes authentication so users log in once and gain access across all connected applications.

features.title: OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0 and SAML 2.0
features.description: Full support for the major identity protocols ensures broad interoperability with existing systems.

features.title: Identity brokering and social login
features.description: Federate authentication through external OIDC/SAML providers or social networks without app code changes.

features.title: LDAP and Active Directory integration
features.description: Built-in connectors let organizations authenticate against existing enterprise directories.

features.title: Fine-grained authorization policies
features.description: Resource- and scope-level permission policies go beyond basic role-based access control.

features.title: Admin and account consoles
features.description: Separate interfaces for administrators managing realms and policies versus end users managing their own accounts.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: Built-in support for multi-factor authentication configurable by end users.

features.title: Self-hosted and Kubernetes-ready
features.description: Deployable on-premises or via containers/Kubernetes for full control over identity infrastructure.

pros: Free and fully open-source with no per-user licensing fees
Full standards support (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, SAML 2.0) for broad interoperability
Native LDAP/Active Directory integration for enterprise environments
CNCF-incubated with vendor-neutral governance since 2023
Backed by an active community and optional Red Hat commercial support
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing operational maintenance
Steeper learning curve than managed SaaS IAM providers
No official managed cloud offering directly from the Keycloak project
Admin console UX can feel dated compared to newer commercial competitors
Scaling to very high traffic requires careful infrastructure tuning

faqs.question: Is Keycloak free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Keycloak is fully open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host, with no per-user licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who created Keycloak?
faqs.answer: Keycloak was co-founded in 2013 by Stian Thorgersen, a Red Hat developer, and has since been donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation as an incubating project.

faqs.question: What protocols does Keycloak support?
faqs.answer: Keycloak fully supports OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0 and SAML 2.0, the major standards for authentication and authorization.

faqs.question: Can Keycloak integrate with Active Directory?
faqs.answer: Yes, Keycloak includes built-in connectors to integrate with existing LDAP or Active Directory servers.

faqs.question: Does Red Hat still maintain Keycloak?
faqs.answer: Red Hat originally stewarded Keycloak and continues to ship a commercially supported distribution, but the upstream project has been governed by the CNCF since April 2023.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Keycloak?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include commercial SaaS providers like Auth0, Okta and Microsoft Entra ID, as well as other self-hostable open-source options like Ory, Authentik and Zitadel.

use_cases.title: Enterprise single sign-on
use_cases.description: Organizations centralize login across internal and customer-facing applications using Keycloak's SSO capabilities.

use_cases.title: API and microservices authorization
use_cases.description: Development teams use Keycloak's OAuth 2.0 and fine-grained authorization to secure APIs and microservice-to-microservice calls.

use_cases.title: Regulated or air-gapped deployments
use_cases.description: Government agencies and regulated industries self-host Keycloak to keep identity data fully within their own infrastructure.

pricing_plans.plan_name: Red Hat build of Keycloak (Commercial Support)

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, multi-threaded fork of Redis built for higher throughput and lower latency while staying protocol-compatible with Redis.
enrichment.seo_title: KeyDB Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KeyDB review covering multi-threading, performance benchmarks, Redis compatibility, and how it compares to Redis, Valkey and Dragonfly.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KeyDB is an open-source, high-performance fork of Redis originally created by John Sully and Ben Schermel at EQ Alpha Technology, and later acquired by Snap Inc. (the parent company of Snapchat) in May 2022, after Snap had already been running KeyDB across a significant portion of its own caching infrastructure. As part of the acquisition, Snap brought the formerly commercial 'KeyDB Pro' features into the open-source codebase and relicensed the project under BSD-3, making the full feature set freely available; the KeyDB team and repository are now maintained as part of Snap Inc., with the project hosted at github.com/Snapchat/KeyDB. KeyDB's central architectural differentiation from standard Redis is multi-threading: where Redis runs a single-threaded event loop for command processing, KeyDB runs the same event loop concurrently across multiple threads, assigning each client connection to a thread on accept() while guarding access to the core hash table with a spinlock, and implements a multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) layer that lets it serve consistent point-in-time snapshots of the dataset without blocking on operations like SCAN or KEYS the way single-threaded Redis can. This design lets a single KeyDB node benchmark over one million operations per second and deliver substantially higher throughput on multi-core hardware, including up to roughly seven times the throughput of Redis when TLS encryption is enabled, since TLS handshake and encryption overhead can also be parallelized across threads. KeyDB maintains full wire-protocol compatibility with Redis, including support for Redis modules and Lua scripts, and supports the same core data structures Redis offers (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, HyperLogLogs, geospatial indexes and streams), positioning itself as a drop-in replacement that requires no client-side code changes for teams migrating from Redis. Beyond raw threading, KeyDB adds features not present in vanilla open-source Redis, including Active Replication (also called multi-master or active-active replication, allowing multiple KeyDB nodes to accept writes and replicate to each other without requiring separate Sentinel nodes for failover), Subkey Expires (allowing individual members within complex data structures like hashes to have their own TTL rather than only whole-key expiration), and ModJS, a JavaScript-based module system built on the V8 engine intended as a faster alternative to Lua for custom scripting. KeyDB also supports cluster-mode sharding for horizontal scale-out with automatic failover, and persistence options comparable to Redis, including periodic RDB-style snapshotting and append-only-file (AOF) command logging, with configurable durability trade-offs. The project has continued to develop additional capabilities such as FLASH-tiered storage (extending the effective in-memory dataset to disk/flash for larger-than-RAM workloads) and multi-tenancy features. Because KeyDB is fully open-source with no separate paid tier, licensing, or subscription, it is used by engineering teams as a free, self-hosted, drop-in Redis alternative for caching, session storage, pub/sub messaging, real-time leaderboards, and other in-memory data use cases where higher per-node throughput on multi-core machines is valuable, without the cost or lock-in of a managed database service. Its primary competitors are Redis itself (including Redis Inc.'s post-2024 relicensed 'Redis' and Redis Enterprise offerings), Valkey (the Linux Foundation-backed, community-governed fork of Redis created after Redis's license change, to which Snap itself migrated its own caching infrastructure roughly two years after acquiring KeyDB), and Dragonfly, another multi-threaded, drop-in Redis-compatible in-memory database built from scratch for modern multi-core hardware. KeyDB differentiates itself from Redis primarily through multi-threaded performance and features like active replication and subkey expiration at zero licensing cost, though its long-term project momentum has been questioned somewhat by the fact that even its own corporate steward, Snap, subsequently moved its production caching workloads to Valkey, the more broadly community-governed fork.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KeyDB is an open-source, multi-threaded fork of Redis originally built by John Sully and Ben Schermel at EQ Alpha Technology and acquired by Snap Inc. in May 2022. Snap had already been running KeyDB across much of its own caching infrastructure before acquiring the team, and as part of the deal, previously commercial 'KeyDB Pro' features were merged into the open-source codebase under a BSD-3 license, making the full feature set free to use.

The project's defining trait is multi-threading: KeyDB runs Redis's core event loop concurrently across multiple CPU threads rather than a single thread, using multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) to serve consistent snapshots without blocking. This lets a single KeyDB node exceed one million operations per second and significantly outperform standard single-threaded Redis on multi-core hardware, while remaining fully protocol-compatible so it can act as a drop-in replacement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond raw throughput, KeyDB adds capabilities not found in standard open-source Redis, including Active Replication for multi-master, active-active setups without needing separate Sentinel nodes, Subkey Expires for setting TTLs on individual members within a hash or other complex structure, and ModJS, a V8-based JavaScript scripting engine offered as a faster alternative to Lua. It also supports cluster-mode sharding with automatic failover for horizontal scaling.

KeyDB supports the same core Redis data structures (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, HyperLogLogs, geospatial indexes, streams) and offers comparable persistence options, including periodic snapshotting and append-only-file logging, giving teams configurable durability without sacrificing Redis compatibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KeyDB is entirely free and open-source, licensed under BSD-3 with no separate commercial tier, subscription, or paid license required to access any feature, including capabilities that were previously gated behind a commercial 'KeyDB Pro' offering before Snap's acquisition folded them into the open codebase.

Because there is no managed cloud product offered directly by the KeyDB project, organizations run it on their own infrastructure (self-hosted, containerized, or on Kubernetes) and costs are limited to compute and operational overhead rather than any licensing fee.

features.title: Multi-threaded architecture
features.description: Runs the Redis event loop concurrently across multiple threads for higher throughput on multi-core hardware.

features.title: Redis protocol compatibility
features.description: Drop-in replacement for Redis supporting the same commands, modules and Lua scripts.

features.title: Active Replication
features.description: Multi-master, active-active replication without requiring separate Sentinel nodes for failover.

features.title: Subkey Expires
features.description: Set individual expiration times on members within hashes and other complex data structures.

features.title: MVCC snapshotting
features.description: Multi-version concurrency control allows non-blocking, consistent point-in-time reads.

features.title: Cluster-mode sharding
features.description: Horizontal scale-out with automatic failover for large deployments.

features.title: ModJS scripting
features.description: V8-based JavaScript module system offered as a faster alternative to Lua scripting.

features.title: TLS performance
features.description: Parallelized TLS handling delivers substantially higher encrypted-throughput than single-threaded Redis.

pros: Completely free and open-source with no paid tier
Significant throughput gains over single-threaded Redis on multi-core hardware
Drop-in Redis compatibility, minimal migration effort
Active-active replication without needing Sentinel
Backed and used in production by Snap Inc.
cons: No official managed cloud/hosted offering from the project itself
Snap itself migrated its own caching infrastructure to Valkey after acquiring KeyDB, raising momentum questions
Smaller community and ecosystem than Redis or Valkey
Some advanced features (FLASH storage, multi-tenancy) are still in development
Requires self-hosting and operational expertise to run at scale

faqs.question: Is KeyDB free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeyDB is fully open-source under a BSD-3 license with no paid tier or licensing fee required for any feature.

faqs.question: Who owns KeyDB?
faqs.answer: KeyDB was created by John Sully and Ben Schermel at EQ Alpha Technology and was acquired by Snap Inc. in May 2022, which now maintains the project.

faqs.question: How is KeyDB different from Redis?
faqs.answer: KeyDB is multi-threaded, running the Redis event loop across multiple CPU cores concurrently, whereas standard Redis is single-threaded, giving KeyDB higher throughput on multi-core hardware while remaining protocol-compatible.

faqs.question: Is KeyDB compatible with existing Redis clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeyDB maintains full Redis protocol compatibility, including support for modules and Lua scripts, making it a drop-in replacement.

faqs.question: What is Active Replication in KeyDB?
faqs.answer: Active Replication is KeyDB's multi-master, active-active replication feature that lets multiple nodes accept writes and replicate to each other without requiring separate Sentinel nodes.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to KeyDB?
faqs.answer: The main alternatives are Redis itself, Valkey (the Linux Foundation-backed community fork of Redis), and Dragonfly, another multi-threaded Redis-compatible in-memory database.

use_cases.title: High-throughput caching
use_cases.description: Applications with heavy read/write caching needs use KeyDB to get higher per-node throughput than single-threaded Redis on multi-core servers.

use_cases.title: Real-time session and leaderboard storage
use_cases.description: Consumer apps use KeyDB's low-latency in-memory data structures for session storage and real-time leaderboards.
use_cases.audience: Product engineering teams at high-traffic consumer apps

use_cases.title: Active-active multi-region deployments
use_cases.description: Teams needing geographically distributed writes use KeyDB's Active Replication to run multi-master setups without Sentinel.
use_cases.audience: Platform and SRE teams operating multi-region infrastructure

enrichment.short_description: Affordable SEO keyword research tool offering keyword difficulty scoring, rank tracking, competitor analysis and site audits for small budgets.
enrichment.seo_title: Keysearch Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keysearch review covering keyword research, rank tracking, pricing plans, and how it compares to Ahrefs and Semrush as a budget SEO tool.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keysearch, marketed at keysearch.co, is a budget-focused SEO keyword research and toolkit platform founded in 2015 by Dan Pfeffer. It has reportedly raised Series A funding of around 4.3 million dollars, though it remains primarily known and marketed as an affordable, all-in-one alternative to enterprise SEO suites rather than as a venture-scale company. The product's core value proposition is consolidating keyword research, keyword difficulty scoring, competitor analysis, rank tracking, site audits and content optimization into a single platform priced at roughly a tenth to a fifth of what comparable enterprise tools like Ahrefs and Semrush charge, targeting solopreneurs, bloggers, affiliate marketers and small businesses rather than agencies or enterprise SEO teams. Functionally, Keysearch's keyword research engine returns search volume, a proprietary keyword difficulty (KD) score, cost-per-click data, and related/long-tail keyword suggestions, and includes a dedicated YouTube keyword research mode for video SEO alongside standard web search. Its competitor analysis tools let users see which keywords a rival domain ranks for and identify content gaps. SERP analysis surfaces the top-ranking pages for a given query along with metrics like backlink counts and on-page signals, helping users gauge realistic ranking difficulty before committing to a keyword. Rank tracking monitors keyword positions over time so users can measure whether content optimization efforts are working, and the Website Audit tool crawls a site to flag technical SEO issues such as broken links, missing metadata and page speed problems. Keysearch also includes an AI-powered recommendation engine called Foresight, which is exclusive to the higher Pro tier, along with a general AI Content Assistant that analyzes top-ranking pages and suggests LSI keywords and semantically related entities to include in on-page content, plus more limited backlink analysis compared to specialized link-building tools. The platform supports multi-language and multi-location keyword research, letting users localize search volume and difficulty data for different countries and languages. Pricing is a simple two-tier monthly subscription model: the Starter plan costs 24 dollars per month and includes 200 keyword searches per day, 80 tracked keywords per month, 2,000 audited pages per month and 5,000 AI credits per month, but does not include the Foresight AI engine; the Pro plan costs 48 dollars per month, is positioned as the most-recommended tier, and adds the Foresight AI recommendation engine along with higher limits of 500 keyword searches per day, 200 tracked keywords per month, 5,000 audited pages per month and 15,000 AI credits per month. Both plans are also available on annual billing, which discounts the cost to roughly two months free compared to paying monthly, and a free trial is offered for new users to evaluate the tool before subscribing. Keysearch's most direct competitors are Ahrefs and Semrush, both of which offer substantially deeper backlink indexes, more comprehensive technical site audits, and broader competitive intelligence, but at a much higher price point, with Semrush's entry plans starting around 165 dollars per month, roughly seven times Keysearch's Starter price. Reviewers generally position Keysearch as a strong fit for bloggers, affiliate marketers and small businesses that need solid core keyword research and basic rank tracking without paying enterprise prices, while acknowledging that its backlink database and overall feature depth do not match Ahrefs or Semrush for teams doing serious link-building or large-scale technical audits. This makes Keysearch's key differentiation cost efficiency and simplicity rather than comprehensiveness, deliberately trading some data depth for a price point accessible to individual creators and small teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keysearch is a budget-oriented SEO keyword research tool founded in 2015 by Dan Pfeffer, built to give bloggers, affiliate marketers and small businesses access to core SEO functionality without the price tag of enterprise suites like Ahrefs or Semrush. The platform consolidates keyword research, competitor analysis, rank tracking and content optimization into a single interface.

Rather than competing on data depth with the largest SEO platforms, Keysearch positions itself around affordability and simplicity, typically priced at a fraction of comparable tools, making it a common recommendation for solo creators and small teams just starting to invest seriously in organic search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Keysearch's keyword research tool surfaces search volume, a proprietary keyword difficulty score, cost-per-click data and related keyword suggestions, with a dedicated mode for YouTube keyword research. Competitor analysis lets users see which keywords a rival domain ranks for, while SERP analysis shows the top-ranking pages for a query along with backlink and on-page signals to gauge realistic difficulty.

Rank tracking monitors keyword position changes over time, the Website Audit tool flags technical SEO issues across a site, and the AI Content Assistant analyzes top-ranking content to suggest related keywords and entities for on-page optimization. The Pro-tier-exclusive Foresight engine adds AI-powered strategic recommendations on top of the core toolset.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Keysearch offers two monthly subscription tiers. Starter costs 24 dollars per month with 200 keyword searches per day, 80 tracked keywords, 2,000 audited pages and 5,000 AI credits monthly, without access to Foresight. Pro costs 48 dollars per month and unlocks the Foresight AI recommendation engine along with higher limits across searches, tracked keywords, audited pages and AI credits.
Both plans can be billed annually at a discount equivalent to roughly two free months, and a free trial is available for prospective users to test the platform before subscribing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source headless CMS and application framework for Node.js that auto-generates a GraphQL API and admin UI from a defined schema.
enrichment.seo_title: KeystoneJS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KeystoneJS review covering its GraphQL API, admin UI, database support, and how it compares to Strapi, Sanity and other headless CMS options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KeystoneJS is an open-source headless content management system and application framework for Node.js, originally created in 2013 by Thinkmill, an Australian design and development consultancy based in Sydney that has built products for clients including Atlassian, Samsung and Qantas. The project was created by Thinkmill developers including Jed Watson, Boris Bozic ('bladey') and Joss Mackison, and continues to be maintained under the MIT license by Thinkmill Labs Pty Ltd alongside an active open-source community; the codebase is hosted publicly on GitHub under the keystonejs organization. KeystoneJS's core architectural idea is schema-first development: developers describe their data model as a schema (fields, relationships, access control rules, and hooks) in TypeScript or JavaScript, and Keystone automatically generates both a fully-featured GraphQL API and an admin UI from that single source of truth, eliminating the need to hand-write CRUD endpoints or build an admin interface from scratch. The current major version, Keystone 6, is built on Prisma for its database layer, giving it out-of-the-box support for PostgreSQL, MySQL and SQLite, along with Prisma-managed schema migrations, in contrast to the earlier Keystone 5/v3 generation, which was built on Express and MongoDB. The generated GraphQL API includes built-in pagination, sorting, filtering, and session-aware queries and mutations, and can be extended with custom resolvers, queries and mutations for application-specific logic beyond the auto-generated CRUD layer. The Admin UI is a React-based, schema-driven management interface that automatically reflects field types and access control rules defined in the schema, and it supports customization through custom React components, a configurable WYSIWYG rich text editor field, and tailored editorial workflows for non-technical content editors. Keystone provides comprehensive field types out of the box (text, relationship, image, password, JSON, select, and more), fine-grained, code-defined access control at the list, field and operation level, session management for authenticated access, and event hooks that let developers run custom logic before or after data operations such as create, update or delete. Because it is a full application framework rather than only a content API, teams use KeystoneJS to build custom web and mobile application backends, dynamic and static websites, e-commerce platforms, and systems that need to distribute content across multiple channels via a single GraphQL API, giving it more backend flexibility than headless CMS products that are purely content-repository-as-a-service. KeystoneJS is entirely free and open-source with no paid tier, hosted plan, or licensing fee from the Keystone project itself; organizations self-host it on their own infrastructure (including standard Node.js hosting, containers, or serverless platforms), and the only costs are hosting, database, and development/operations effort, since there is no official managed cloud offering. This puts it in a different pricing category from commercial hosted headless CMS platforms such as Contentful, Sanity and Strapi Cloud, which typically charge based on usage, seats or API calls; KeystoneJS's closest self-hosted, open-source peers are Strapi (which also offers a self-hosted community edition) and Directus, both of which similarly auto-generate CRUD APIs and admin interfaces from a schema. KeystoneJS differentiates itself from these competitors primarily through its GraphQL-first API design (rather than REST-first, as with Strapi's default configuration), its tight Prisma-based database integration and migration tooling, and its origins as a framework built by a consultancy with deep enterprise product delivery experience, making it a common choice for TypeScript-first engineering teams that want full backend and admin-UI control without building CRUD infrastructure from scratch, and who are comfortable self-hosting rather than paying for a managed content-API vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KeystoneJS is an open-source headless CMS and application framework for Node.js, originally created in 2013 by Thinkmill, a Sydney-based design and development consultancy whose developers include Jed Watson, Boris Bozic and Joss Mackison. The project remains actively maintained under the MIT license by Thinkmill Labs and its open-source community.

Keystone's defining approach is schema-first development: developers define their data model once, in code, and Keystone auto-generates both a GraphQL API and an admin UI from that schema. The current major version, Keystone 6, is built on Prisma, supporting PostgreSQL, MySQL and SQLite with managed schema migrations, replacing the Express/MongoDB stack used by earlier Keystone versions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The auto-generated GraphQL API includes built-in pagination, sorting, filtering and session-aware queries, and can be extended with custom resolvers and mutations for application-specific logic. The React-based Admin UI reflects the defined schema and access control rules automatically, and supports custom React components and a configurable WYSIWYG rich text editor for tailored editorial workflows.

Keystone provides a comprehensive set of field types, fine-grained code-defined access control at the list, field and operation level, session management, and event hooks for running custom logic around data operations, making it a full application backend framework rather than just a content repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KeystoneJS is entirely free and open-source with no paid tier or licensing fee from the project itself; there is no official managed hosting offering, so teams self-host on their own infrastructure, whether traditional servers, containers, or serverless platforms.

Because there is no vendor subscription, costs are limited to hosting, database and operational effort, distinguishing Keystone's pricing model from commercial hosted headless CMS platforms like Contentful or Sanity, which charge based on usage, seats or API call volume.

Migration from older Keystone (v5/Express/MongoDB) versions requires significant rework

faqs.question: Is KeystoneJS free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeystoneJS is fully open-source under the MIT license with no paid tier or licensing fee.

faqs.answer: KeystoneJS was created in 2013 by Thinkmill, a Sydney-based design and development consultancy, and continues to be maintained by Thinkmill Labs and the open-source community.

faqs.answer: Keystone 6 is built on Prisma and supports PostgreSQL, MySQL and SQLite with managed schema migrations.

faqs.question: Does KeystoneJS offer managed hosting?
faqs.answer: No, there is no official managed hosting offering from the Keystone project; teams self-host on their own infrastructure.

faqs.question: How does KeystoneJS generate its API?
faqs.answer: Keystone auto-generates a full GraphQL API, including pagination, sorting and filtering, directly from a code-defined schema.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to KeystoneJS?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Strapi and Directus, both of which similarly auto-generate CRUD APIs and admin interfaces from a schema, as well as hosted platforms like Contentful and Sanity.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered keyword clustering and content strategy platform that groups keywords by search intent using live SERP data and generates content briefs.
enrichment.seo_title: Keyword Insights Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keyword Insights review covering AI keyword clustering, content briefs, pricing tiers, and how it compares to Surfer SEO and Clearscope.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keyword Insights is an AI-powered keyword clustering and content strategy platform founded in 2021 by Andreas Voniatis and Suganthan Mohanadasan, both established figures in the SEO industry, and operated under the registered UK trademark Keyword Insights, trading through the legal entity Snippet Digital Ltd. The product's core function is automating one of the most time-consuming parts of SEO content strategy: grouping large keyword lists into search-intent-based clusters. Users upload a list of up to 50,000 keywords, and the platform runs live SERP analysis on each keyword, then uses an AI clustering algorithm to group keywords that share the same ranking pages and search intent signals, producing topic clusters that reflect what Google is actually rewarding rather than relying purely on semantic or lexical similarity between keyword strings. Since its launch, Keyword Insights has expanded from a pure clustering tool into a fuller content workflow platform, adding AI-generated content briefs that outline recommended structure, subtopics, and entities for a target cluster, an AI Writing Agent that can produce full draft articles, and a separate writing assistant feature for in-line content optimization while drafting. The company has built a reputation among SEO agencies and in-house content marketing teams specifically for the accuracy of its clustering algorithm compared to competitors, positioning it as a research and planning layer that sits upstream of content production and on-page optimization tools. Pricing is structured around a monthly or discounted annual subscription with a credit-based consumption model layered on top of each tier: the Basic plan costs 58 dollars per month and includes 10,000 credits, 1 user seat, 5 workspaces, and AI agent support; the Professional plan, marketed as the most popular tier, costs 99 dollars per month and includes 20,000 credits, 3 user seats, 10 workspaces, and adds team-sharing capability; and an Enterprise tier offers custom credit allocations, custom seat counts, custom workspace limits, priority support, and guided onboarding, with pricing available only on request. Within any plan, credits are consumed per action rather than per keyword list: keyword clustering costs 1 credit per keyword processed, generating a content brief costs 100 credits, using the writing assistant costs 200 credits per article, and running the full AI Writing Agent to draft a complete article costs 1,200 credits. New users can also access a 7-day trial for a one-time cost of 1 dollar, which includes 5,000 one-time credits and full feature access, automatically converting to a pay-as-you-go arrangement if not upgraded to a full plan after the trial period ends. Keyword Insights' target audience is SEO agencies, in-house content marketing teams, and content strategists who need to plan large-scale content programs efficiently, particularly organizations dealing with keyword lists too large to cluster manually or via simple semantic-similarity tools. Its most direct competitors are content optimization and topical-planning platforms such as Surfer SEO and Clearscope, both of which focus more on scoring and optimizing individual pieces of content against top-ranking competitors, whereas Keyword Insights focuses further upstream on clustering and content strategy before a single article is written; Surfer SEO in particular has built out a broader end-to-end AI content workflow (including features like Auto Optimize and content gap analysis) at a similar or slightly higher price point starting around 69 dollars per month, while Clearscope targets larger enterprise content teams starting around 170 dollars per month with an emphasis on simplicity and writer adoption over comprehensive SEO control. Keyword Insights differentiates itself by using live SERP data as the basis for clustering (grouping keywords that share actual ranking pages, not just similar wording), by supporting very large keyword list sizes, and by pricing that positions it as more accessible than Clearscope while still targeting professional and agency-scale SEO workflows rather than casual bloggers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keyword Insights is an AI-powered keyword clustering and content strategy platform founded in 2021 by SEO industry figures Andreas Voniatis and Suganthan Mohanadasan, operating under the legal entity Snippet Digital Ltd. The platform automates the process of grouping large keyword lists into search-intent-based clusters using live SERP data.

Users can upload up to 50,000 keywords at once, and the platform's AI clustering algorithm groups keywords that share the same ranking pages and intent signals, rather than relying only on lexical or semantic similarity. Since launch, the product has expanded beyond pure clustering into a broader content workflow, adding AI content briefs, a writing assistant, and a full AI Writing Agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core clustering engine analyzes live SERP data for each uploaded keyword and groups terms that share ranking pages, producing intent-based clusters that reflect actual search engine behavior. On top of clustering, the platform generates AI content briefs outlining recommended structure and subtopics for each cluster, and offers a writing assistant for in-line optimization plus a full AI Writing Agent capable of drafting complete articles.

The platform supports team collaboration through shared workspaces and multi-seat plans on its higher tiers, making it suited to agencies and larger in-house content teams that need to coordinate large-scale content planning across multiple stakeholders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Keyword Insights uses a credit-based subscription model. The Basic plan costs 58 dollars per month for 10,000 credits, 1 seat and 5 workspaces; the Professional plan costs 99 dollars per month for 20,000 credits, 3 seats and 10 workspaces with team sharing; and Enterprise offers custom credits, seats and workspaces with priority support at custom pricing.

Credits are consumed per action: 1 credit per keyword clustered, 100 credits per content brief, 200 credits per writing-assistant article, and 1,200 credits per AI Writing Agent article. A 7-day trial is available for 1 dollar with 5,000 one-time credits, auto-converting to pay-as-you-go if not upgraded.

enrichment.short_description: Free nonprofit online education platform offering thousands of lessons, courses and an AI tutor covering math, science and more from preK through college.
languages: and dozens of other languages via international partners
enrichment.seo_title: Khan Academy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Khan Academy review covering free courses, subjects, the Khanmigo AI tutor, funding model, and how it compares to Coursera and Duolingo.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Khan Academy is an American nonprofit educational organization headquartered in Mountain View, California, founded by Salman 'Sal' Khan. Its origins trace back to 2004, when Khan began remotely tutoring a cousin in math using online tools, which grew into a library of instructional videos he shared for free; he continued building the project in his spare time while working at a hedge fund until 2009, when he left that job to work on it full-time, funded initially by his own savings and then by an early donation from philanthropist Ann Doerr. Khan Academy was formally incorporated as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization in 2008. It gained major early momentum in September 2010 when it received large grants from Google (2 million dollars) and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (1.5 million dollars), which allowed Khan to build out a full organization beyond himself. Since then, the organization has been sustained primarily through philanthropic donations rather than tuition or subscription revenue, with supporters over the years including the Gates Foundation, Google, Bank of America, the Amgen Foundation, Ann and John Doerr, the Musk Foundation, Stand Together Trust, Reed Hastings, the Overdeck Family Foundation, and others, keeping its core educational content free for all users. Khan Academy's platform delivers instructional content across preK-2 literacy and math, grades 3 through college-level math and science, financial literacy, and standardized test preparation (including SAT prep), organized into more than 7,000 lessons and over 400 supplementary books/resources, taught through short instructional videos paired with practice exercises, quizzes and mastery-based progress tracking that adapts to each learner's pace. In its most recent reported school year (SY24-25), Khan Academy reported reaching 62.4 million international learners, educators and parents, alongside 54.2 million 'grassroots' users outside of formal international district partnerships, and 189.6 million total registered users, logging 66.8 billion cumulative learning minutes on the platform. The organization also partners directly with school districts internationally to integrate its content into formal curricula, and continually expands its course catalog, releasing 24 new or expanded courses in SY24-25 alone, including a Middle School Chemistry course, SAT prep courses, and a Constitution 101 course. A major recent product expansion is Khanmigo, Khan Academy's AI-powered tutor and teaching assistant, which uses large language model technology to provide personalized, Socratic-style tutoring to students and lesson-planning and grading assistance to teachers rather than simply giving away answers. Khanmigo usage grew dramatically between the 2024-25 and 2025-26 school years, from roughly 40,000 users to about 700,000 users, more than a seventeenfold increase, although some reporting has noted that only a fraction of students with access to Khanmigo actively use it, prompting Khan Academy to redesign aspects of the tool. Because Khan Academy is a nonprofit funded by donations and grants rather than a commercial company charging subscription fees, its core content and courses remain free to all users, which sharply differentiates its business and access model from for-profit education competitors such as Coursera, Udemy, and Duolingo, which rely on paid subscriptions, course fees, or freemium upsells for revenue; Khan Academy instead monetizes indirectly through philanthropic funding and district partnerships while keeping the end-user experience free. Its differentiation from these competitors is therefore less about product depth in any single subject and more about being a universally free, broad-curriculum, K-12-and-beyond learning platform trusted by schools, backed by major philanthropic institutions, and increasingly augmented by AI tutoring through Khanmigo, positioning it as core infrastructure for supplemental and remedial education worldwide rather than a paid consumer education product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Khan Academy is a nonprofit online education platform founded by Sal Khan, with origins tracing back to 2004 when he began tutoring a cousin remotely using online tools, and formally incorporated as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit in 2008 after Khan left his hedge fund job to pursue it full time. Headquartered in Mountain View, California, the organization is funded through philanthropic donations and major grants rather than user fees, keeping its educational content free for everyone.

Early funding from Google and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation in 2010 allowed Khan Academy to grow from a one-person video library into a full nonprofit organization. It now reaches tens of millions of learners globally each year, delivering thousands of lessons across math, science, literacy and test preparation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Khan Academy's platform offers more than 7,000 lessons and 400+ supplementary books spanning preK-2 literacy and math, grades 3 through college-level math and science, financial literacy, and standardized test prep including SAT courses. Content is delivered through short instructional videos combined with practice exercises and mastery-based progress tracking that adapts to each learner's pace.

Its newest major feature is Khanmigo, an AI-powered tutor built on large language model technology that provides Socratic-style personalized tutoring for students and lesson-planning/grading support for teachers. Khanmigo usage grew roughly seventeenfold between the 2024-25 and 2025-26 school years, reflecting rapid adoption as Khan Academy integrates AI more deeply into its offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Khan Academy's core educational content is entirely free to use for all learners, teachers and parents, reflecting its nonprofit mission and funding model based on donations and grants rather than subscriptions or course fees.

The organization sustains itself through philanthropic support from major donors and foundations, along with district partnerships for institutional adoption, rather than by charging end users, which distinguishes it from for-profit online education platforms that rely on paid subscriptions.

faqs.question: What subjects does Khan Academy cover?
faqs.answer: Khan Academy covers preK-2 literacy and math, grades 3 through college-level math and science, financial literacy, and standardized test prep including SAT courses.

faqs.question: How many users does Khan Academy have?
faqs.answer: Khan Academy reported 189.6 million total registered users and 62.4 million international learners, educators and parents reached in the SY24-25 school year.

faqs.question: How is Khan Academy funded?
faqs.answer: Khan Academy is funded primarily through philanthropic donations and grants from foundations and individuals, including early support from Google and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.

use_cases.title: Supplemental K-12 learning
use_cases.description: Students use Khan Academy to reinforce classroom learning in math and science at their own pace outside school hours.

use_cases.title: Classroom integration
use_cases.description: Teachers and school districts integrate Khan Academy content and Khanmigo into formal curricula for personalized instruction.

use_cases.title: Standardized test preparation
use_cases.description: Students use Khan Academy's free SAT prep courses to study for college entrance exams without paying for tutoring.
use_cases.audience: High school students preparing for SAT

features: Available to individuals, teachers and schools

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable AI second brain that lets users chat with their notes and the web, build custom agents and automate research.
enrichment.seo_title: Khoj Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Khoj review covering its open-source AI second brain, self-hosting, supported platforms, and how it compares to NotebookLM and Obsidian AI plugins.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Khoj is an open-source, self-hostable personal AI application founded by Debanjum Singh and Saba Imran, positioned as a 'second brain' that lets individuals search, chat with, and build automated workflows around their own notes and documents alongside the open web. The project is backed by Y Combinator and has raised a reported 500,000 dollars in seed funding as of its most recent disclosed round in 2023; the small team has been reported at around three employees as of early 2026, reflecting its identity as a lean, community-driven open-source project rather than a large venture-scale company. Khoj is distributed under the AGPL-3.0 open-source license and hosted publicly on GitHub, where it has accumulated tens of thousands of stars (reported around 35.9k), indicating substantial developer community interest. Architecturally, Khoj combines retrieval-augmented generation with semantic search: it indexes a user's personal documents (including images, PDFs, Markdown, Org-mode files, Word documents, and Notion exports) into embeddings, then lets users ask natural-language questions and receive answers grounded in the relevant parts of their own files even when the query doesn't share exact keywords with the source text, functioning similarly in concept to Google's NotebookLM but built to be run entirely under the user's own control. Beyond document search, Khoj supports building custom AI agents with their own persona, knowledge base, chat model and tool access, so users can create purpose-built assistants for different roles or projects. It can schedule automated research tasks and smart notifications, generate images, and offer text-to-speech playback of responses. A key differentiator is model flexibility: Khoj can be configured to run against local, on-device open models (such as Llama 3, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral or DeepSeek) or against hosted commercial models (including GPT, Claude and Gemini), letting users choose their own privacy, cost and performance trade-offs rather than being locked to a single AI provider. Khoj is accessible across a wide range of surfaces, including a web browser interface, a desktop application, a phone/mobile interface, and native integrations for the note-taking tools Obsidian and Emacs, plus a WhatsApp-based chat interface, making it usable directly inside a user's existing note-taking and messaging workflows rather than requiring a separate destination app. The self-hosted version of Khoj is entirely free aside from the user's own hardware and infrastructure costs, consistent with its open-source, privacy-first positioning; the company previously operated a hosted cloud version at app.khoj.dev offering a free trial and paid plans, including discounted pricing for students and academics, but that official hosted cloud service has since been deprecated/shut down, leaving self-hosting (on personal hardware or via third-party platforms like Railway) as the primary way to run Khoj today. The company has also introduced adjacent products under its broader mission of building transparent, trustworthy AI, including Open Paper, a research-paper reading and citation-verification tool, and Pipali, a beta AI co-worker product for research and content-creation tasks that runs locally. Khoj's most direct competitors are Google's NotebookLM, which offers a more polished, tightly integrated retrieval-augmented-generation experience but is a closed, hosted-only Google product without self-hosting or open-source options; Obsidian, which is not itself an AI product but becomes a comparable AI-powered 'second brain' when paired with community plugins like Copilot, Smart Connections or Text Generator; and Mem and Recall, both AI-oriented note and knowledge-management platforms. Khoj differentiates itself from all of these primarily on openness and control: it is fully open-source, self-hostable, and provider-agnostic on which LLM it uses, appealing specifically to privacy-conscious individuals, developers and organizations who want a NotebookLM-like personal knowledge assistant without depending on a single closed vendor or sending their documents to a third-party cloud service by default.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Khoj is an open-source, self-hostable personal AI application founded by Debanjum Singh and Saba Imran, backed by Y Combinator, and built as an 'AI second brain' that lets users search and chat with their own notes and documents alongside the open web. The project is distributed under the AGPL-3.0 license and has attracted a large open-source community, with tens of thousands of GitHub stars.

Functionally similar in concept to Google's NotebookLM, Khoj indexes personal documents into semantic embeddings and answers natural-language questions grounded in the user's own files, while additionally supporting custom AI agents, scheduled research automations, image generation and text-to-speech, all of which can run against either local open models or hosted commercial LLMs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Khoj indexes a wide range of document types, including images, PDFs, Markdown, Org-mode, Word documents and Notion exports, and lets users query them conversationally through semantic search. Users can build custom agents with distinct personas, knowledge bases, chat models and tool access, and schedule automated research tasks with smart notifications.

The platform is model-agnostic, supporting both local on-device models (Llama 3, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral, DeepSeek) and hosted commercial models (GPT, Claude, Gemini), giving users control over privacy and cost trade-offs. Khoj is accessible via browser, desktop app, mobile, and native integrations for Obsidian, Emacs, and WhatsApp.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Khoj's self-hosted version is free and open-source, aside from the user's own hardware or hosting costs, reflecting its privacy-first, community-driven design. The project's official hosted cloud service, previously offered at app.khoj.dev with a free trial and paid plans including student discounts, has since been deprecated.

Users who want a cloud-hosted deployment today typically self-host on third-party infrastructure such as Railway, rather than through an officially maintained Khoj-run cloud service, since the company no longer offers its own hosted subscription.

features.title: Semantic document search
features.description: Indexes personal documents (PDFs, images, Markdown, Word, Notion) into embeddings for natural-language search and chat.

features.title: Custom AI agents
features.description: Build agents with their own persona, knowledge base, chat model and tool access for specific tasks or roles.

features.title: Model-agnostic LLM support
features.description: Runs against local open models (Llama, Qwen, Mistral) or hosted commercial models (GPT, Claude, Gemini).

features.title: Scheduled research automation
features.description: Automates recurring research tasks and delivers smart notifications.

features.title: Multi-platform access
features.description: Available via browser, desktop app, mobile, and native Obsidian and Emacs integrations.

features.title: WhatsApp integration
features.description: Chat with Khoj directly through WhatsApp for on-the-go access.

features.title: Image generation and text-to-speech
features.description: Generates images and provides spoken playback of AI responses.

features.title: Self-hostable and open-source
features.description: Fully self-hostable under AGPL-3.0, giving users full control over their data and infrastructure.

pros: Fully open-source and self-hostable for complete data control
Model-agnostic, supporting both local and commercial LLMs
Wide platform reach, including Obsidian, Emacs and WhatsApp integrations
Free to self-host aside from hardware/infrastructure costs
Backed by Y Combinator with an active open-source community
cons: Official hosted cloud service has been deprecated/shut down
Self-hosting requires technical setup and infrastructure management
Small team (reportedly around 3 employees) limits support resources
Retrieval accuracy can trail more polished closed products like NotebookLM
Documentation and setup complexity may deter non-technical users

faqs.question: Is Khoj free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted version of Khoj is free and open-source, aside from the user's own hardware or hosting costs. The official hosted cloud service has since been deprecated.

faqs.question: Who created Khoj?
faqs.answer: Khoj was founded by Debanjum Singh and Saba Imran, and is backed by Y Combinator.

faqs.question: What file types can Khoj search?
faqs.answer: Khoj can index and search images, PDFs, Markdown, Org-mode files, Word documents and Notion exports, alongside general web search.

faqs.question: Can Khoj run with local AI models?
faqs.answer: Yes, Khoj supports both local open models such as Llama 3, Qwen, Gemma and Mistral, and hosted commercial models like GPT, Claude and Gemini.

faqs.question: What platforms does Khoj support?
faqs.answer: Khoj is accessible via web browser, desktop app, mobile, and has native integrations with Obsidian, Emacs and WhatsApp.

faqs.question: How does Khoj compare to Google's NotebookLM?
faqs.answer: Khoj offers similar retrieval-augmented-generation functionality over personal documents, but unlike NotebookLM it is open-source, self-hostable and model-agnostic rather than a closed, hosted-only Google product.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge management
use_cases.description: Individuals use Khoj to search and chat with their own notes, documents and research across multiple file formats.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused AI assistance
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious users self-host Khoj with local LLMs to keep documents and queries entirely off third-party servers.

use_cases.title: Automated research workflows
use_cases.description: Users schedule recurring research tasks and custom agents to monitor topics and summarize findings automatically.
